event: Austronesian voyagers associated with the Lapita cultural complex reach the Samoan archipelago, marking the first human settlement of the islands.
Lapita pottery is produced at coastal sites such as Mulifanua on Upolu, the oldest dated archaeological evidence of human presence in Samoa.
Distinctive Polynesian society begins to develop in Samoa as Lapita pottery traditions fade and a localized maritime culture emerges.
Samoa becomes a key hearth of ancestral Polynesian culture, with voyagers from the archipelago helping seed the settlement of eastern Polynesia.
Tongan expansion under the Tu'i Tonga maritime empire extends political and cultural influence over parts of Samoa.
Samoan chiefs led by Malietoa Savea expel the Tongan overlords, an event commemorated in the Malietoa title meaning 'brave warrior.'
The fa'amatai chiefly system and the four paramount papa titles consolidate, organizing Samoan society into competing political confederations.
Dutch explorer Jacob Roggeveen sights the islands of Samoa, becoming the first recorded European to encounter the archipelago.
French navigator Louis-Antoine de Bougainville visits and names the group the Navigator Islands, impressed by the Samoans' skill with canoes.
A deadly clash between the expedition of French explorer La Perouse and Samoans at Tutuila leaves several Europeans and many Samoans dead.
London Missionary Society missionary John Williams arrives at Sapapali'i, beginning the rapid Christianization of Samoa.
Christianity spreads across the islands as missionary churches, literacy, and new social norms reshape Samoan village life.
The German trading firm Godeffroy establishes a base at Apia, expanding commercial coconut and copra plantations in Samoa.
Civil wars among rival paramount chiefs intensify as Britain, Germany, and the United States vie for influence over Samoa.
The United States signs a treaty securing rights to a naval station at Pago Pago harbor on Tutuila.
A devastating cyclone wrecks German and American warships at Apia during a tense standoff, prompting the Treaty of Berlin to neutralize Samoa.
Author Robert Louis Stevenson, who had settled at Vailima near Apia, dies and is buried on Mount Vaea overlooking the town.
The Tripartite Convention partitions Samoa, granting the western islands to Germany and the eastern islands to the United States.
Germany formally annexes Western Samoa, while the United States takes control of Tutuila and the eastern islands as American Samoa.
The Mau a Pule resistance movement emerges in German Samoa, voicing early organized opposition to colonial rule.
New Zealand forces seize German Samoa at the outbreak of World War I, beginning a long period of New Zealand administration.
The influenza pandemic, introduced via the ship Talune, kills roughly a fifth of Western Samoa's population due to colonial negligence.
New Zealand receives a League of Nations mandate to govern Western Samoa as a trust territory.
New Zealand police fire on a peaceful Mau independence procession in Apia on Black Saturday, killing paramount chief Tupua Tamasese Lealofi III and others.
United States Marines establish bases in American Samoa during the Pacific theatre of World War II, transforming the local economy.
Western Samoa becomes a United Nations Trust Territory under New Zealand administration after World War II.
A Legislative Assembly is established in Western Samoa, expanding Samoan participation in self-government.
Western Samoa adopts a constitution blending the fa'amatai chiefly system with parliamentary democracy ahead of independence.
Western Samoa becomes the first Pacific island nation to gain independence, with Malietoa Tanumafili II and Tupua Tamasese Mea'ole as joint heads of state.
Western Samoa joins the Commonwealth of Nations, deepening ties with other former British and New Zealand territories.
Western Samoa is admitted to the United Nations as a full member state.
A national referendum approves universal suffrage, extending the right to vote beyond matai title-holders to all adult citizens.
Cyclone Val devastates Western Samoa, causing widespread destruction and significant loss of life.
Western Samoa officially shortens its name to Samoa, prompting protests from neighboring American Samoa.
New Zealand Prime Minister Helen Clark formally apologizes to Samoa for colonial-era injustices, including the 1918 influenza disaster and Black Saturday.
Tuiatua Tupua Tamasese Efi becomes head of state following the death of Malietoa Tanumafili II, ending the lifetime joint-headship era.
Samoa switches from driving on the right to the left side of the road to align with Australia and New Zealand.
An undersea earthquake triggers a tsunami that strikes Samoa, American Samoa, and Tonga, killing more than 180 people.
Samoa jumps westward across the International Date Line, skipping December 30 to better synchronize trade with Australasia.
Cyclone Evan strikes Samoa, causing severe flooding, deaths, and major damage to Apia and infrastructure.
Apia hosts the United Nations Conference on Small Island Developing States, spotlighting climate-change threats to low-lying Pacific nations.
A measles epidemic kills more than 80 people, mostly young children, exposing gaps in Samoa's vaccination coverage.
Fiame Naomi Mata'afa becomes Samoa's first female prime minister after a contested election and constitutional standoff.
Samoa continues to advocate at international forums for climate action and adaptation funding as rising seas threaten its coasts.
Samoa hosts the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting in Apia, the first time the summit is held in a Pacific island nation.
